execSummaryBullets: Jahez International Company (Tadawul: 6017) is a Saudi-listed quick-commerce and logistics group. Following the Q4-2025 consolidation of Snoonu, the group operates across KSA food delivery, non-KSA delivery, and last-mile logistics; the Logistics segment recorded {fy25.segment_logistics_net_revenue} of FY2025 net revenue.

FY2025 GMV reached {fy25.gmv} (+{calc.gmvGrowthFy25} YoY) on {fy25.orders} orders (+{calc.ordersGrowthFy25} YoY). Net revenue grew to {fy25.net_revenue} (+{calc.netRevGrowthFy25} YoY) at a {fy25.take_rate} take rate, up from {fy24.take_rate} in FY2024A.

Reported profitability contracted. Net income declined {calc.netIncomeDeclineFy25Magnitude} YoY to {fy25.net_income}, of which {fy25.one_off} traces to identified Q4-2025 one-off items (Red Color impairment, Marn goodwill impairment, Others-segment expected credit losses, and Snoonu transaction costs). Q1-2026 recorded a {calc.q1LossMagnitude} net loss while adjusted EBITDA remained positive at {q1_26.adj_ebitda}.

Adjusted EBITDA margin contracted approximately {calc.ebitdaMarginContractionBps} bps to {fy25.adj_ebitda_margin} (FY2024A: {fy24.adj_ebitda_margin}); gross margin declined to {fy25.gross_margin} from {fy24.gross_margin}. Both movements are consistent with absorbed competitive pricing intensity in KSA delivery rather than structural demand weakness; the distinction is tested in the scenario analysis below.

Faheem's valuation model rates the position {calc.rating}, with a DCF-anchored target price of {calc.targetPrice} per share against the {calc.currentPrice} close, implying {calc.upside} upside and a base-case four-year IRR of {calc.irrBase}.

The DCF discounts firm free cash flow at a {calc.wacc} WACC ({calc.costOfEquity} cost of equity, {calc.beta} comparable-set beta) against {calc.terminalGrowth} terminal growth. Trading-comparables triangulation across Talabat, DoorDash, and Delivery Hero spans {calc.compsMin} to {calc.compsMax} of implied value per share (median {calc.compsMedian}).

Scenario analysis weights bull, base, and bear cases at 25% / 50% / 25%, producing a probability-weighted value of {calc.weightedPerShare} per share and an expected return of {calc.weightedReturn} against Lunar's {calc.hurdle} mandate hurdle. The bear case returns {calc.irrBear} and does not clear the hurdle in isolation.

The balance sheet carries {calc.netCash} of net cash. The AAOIFI-style compliance screen passes on both financial ratios: interest-bearing debt at {calc.debtRatio} of market capitalisation and cash at {calc.cashRatio}, each inside the 33.00% threshold.

The composite quantified risk score is {calc.riskScore} out of 10; the dominant exposure is price-war margin compression. This memorandum is advisory only; the investment decision rests with the Investment Committee.

thesisPillars.title: Logistics diversification de-risks the single-platform delivery model
thesisPillars.body: The Snoonu acquisition (Q4-2025 consolidation) is the strategic pivot: FY2025 Snoonu GMV of {fy25.snoonu_gmv} contributed {fy25.snoonu_gmv_contribution} to Group GMV from October, and delivered {fy25.snoonu_adj_ebitda} of profitable adjusted EBITDA in its own right, the Logistics segment reached {fy25.segment_logistics_net_revenue} of FY2025 net revenue. Group orders grew {calc.ordersGrowthFy25} YoY to {fy25.orders}, and GMV {calc.gmvGrowthFy25} to {fy25.gmv}, the platform is still winning volume even as the KSA delivery wars compress unit economics. Diversification beyond single-app KSA food delivery is the structural offset to the price-war risk quantified below.

thesisPillars.title: Margin recovery is underway, not derailed, despite a headline loss-making quarter
thesisPillars.body: FY2025 adjusted EBITDA of {fy25.adj_ebitda} ({fy25.adj_ebitda_margin} margin) stepped down from FY2024A's {fy24.adj_ebitda} ({fy24.adj_ebitda_margin}) almost entirely on the {fy25.one_off} of identified Q4 one-offs, impairments and deal costs, not operating deterioration, and Q1-2026 adjusted EBITDA of {q1_26.adj_ebitda} shows the operating engine still generating positive cash profit through the net-loss quarter. The scenario-weighted return of {calc.weightedReturn} already prices in the risk that margin recovery slips, and it still clears the {calc.hurdle} hurdle.

thesisPillars.title: A Compliance-compliant, asset-light, net-cash balance sheet fits the mandate cleanly
thesisPillars.body: Jahez screens {calc.complianceStatus} on both AAOIFI-style ratios, interest-bearing debt at {calc.debtRatio} of market cap and cash at {calc.cashRatio}, both inside the 33% threshold, while carrying {calc.netCash} of net cash against {q1_26.islamic_facilities_loans} of Islamic facilities and loans (Q1 2026 Financial Statements). The business model requires minimal fixed capital, and the Tadawul-listed public equity satisfies Lunar's liquidity requirement for the position (Lunar IC Charter, p.4).

strengths.title: Diversification beyond single-vertical delivery
strengths.body: Snoonu contributed {fy25.snoonu_gmv_contribution} to Group GMV from an October 2025 consolidation start against full-year Snoonu GMV of {fy25.snoonu_gmv}, and generated {fy25.snoonu_adj_ebitda} of adjusted EBITDA in its own right. The Logistics segment reached {fy25.segment_logistics_net_revenue} of FY2025 net revenue, reducing dependence on single-app KSA food delivery.

strengths.title: Volume growth sustained through a price war
strengths.body: Orders grew {calc.ordersGrowthFy25} YoY to {fy25.orders} and GMV grew {calc.gmvGrowthFy25} to {fy25.gmv} in FY2025A, with Q1-2026 orders of {q1_26.orders} on GMV of {q1_26.gmv}. The platform continued to add volume through a period of elevated competitive intensity in KSA delivery pricing.

strengths.title: Rising monetisation of the GMV base
strengths.body: Take rate rose to {fy25.take_rate} in FY2025A from {fy24.take_rate} in FY2024A and {fy23.take_rate} in FY2023A, lifting commission revenue to {fy25.commission_revenue} (+{fy25.commission_revenue_growth} YoY). Monetisation per unit of GMV has expanded through the competitive cycle to date.

strengths.title: A profitable KSA core funding the growth segments
strengths.body: The KSA delivery segment generated {fy25.segment_ksa_adj_ebitda} of adjusted EBITDA on {fy25.segment_ksa_net_revenue} of net revenue in FY2025A, while Non-KSA ({fy25.segment_nonksa_adj_ebitda}) and Others ({fy25.segment_others_adj_ebitda}) remain loss-making or sub-scale. The core franchise carries the group P&L while the diversification builds.

strengths.title: Net-cash, compliance-eligible balance sheet with listed liquidity
strengths.body: The group carries {calc.netCash} of net cash against {q1_26.islamic_facilities_loans} of Islamic facilities and loans, passes both AAOIFI-style screening ratios ({calc.debtRatio} debt and {calc.cashRatio} cash against market capitalisation), and trades on Tadawul with active daily liquidity, satisfying the mandate's liquidity requirement.

concerns.title: Price-war margin compression
concerns.body: Discount-led competition, led by the well-funded entrant Keeta, has already compressed adjusted EBITDA margin by approximately {calc.ebitdaMarginContractionBps} bps to {fy25.adj_ebitda_margin}. Continued fee subsidisation across the sector could delay or flatten the assumed margin-recovery path; this is the highest-scored risk on the register and the primary bear-case driver.

concerns.title: Further well-capitalised entrants
concerns.body: Beyond the current field, additional funded platforms (Rabbit, the Ninja unicorn, Dingdong) are adding fulfilment-density pressure in KSA quick-commerce (Industry & News Pack, p.2). Each new entrant extends the period over which pricing, rider supply, and marketing costs stay elevated.

concerns.title: Earnings volatility and one-off charges
concerns.body: Q4-2025 carried {fy25.one_off} of one-off items across four distinct categories, and Q1-2026 printed a {calc.q1LossMagnitude} net loss. A recurrence of impairments of similar size would indicate the charges are structural rather than one-time, and would warrant re-running the scenario weights.

concerns.title: Margin-recovery execution risk
concerns.body: The forecast rebuilds adjusted EBITDA margin from {fy25.adj_ebitda_margin} in FY2025A toward the FY2024A level of {fy24.adj_ebitda_margin} by the terminal year, anchored to management's FY2026 guidance. Slippage against that path feeds directly through to the DCF value; the bear case ({calc.irrBear} IRR at {calc.perShareBear} per share) quantifies the downside.

concerns.title: Valuation sensitivity and market-data ambiguity
concerns.body: The DCF value is sensitive to the WACC-minus-growth spread (see the workbook sensitivity grid), and the public share-count record carries a known 203m vs 217m discrepancy pending Tadawul register reconciliation (Market Data & Comparables Snapshot, p.2). Both are handled with conservative settings rather than assumed away.

memo.companyIndustry: Jahez operates in Saudi Arabia's quick-commerce and food-delivery market against HungerStation, the well-funded new entrant Keeta, and a wave of further well-capitalised players, Rabbit, the Ninja unicorn, and Dingdong, competing primarily on delivery-fee discounting rather than product differentiation (Industry & News Pack · Saudi Quick-Commerce, p.2). The competitive response has been consolidation and diversification: Jahez's Q4-2025 acquisition of logistics platform Snoonu extends the group beyond single-vertical food delivery into broader last-mile logistics, a segment that posted {fy25.segment_logistics_net_revenue} of FY2025 net revenue. Group take rate rose to {fy25.take_rate} in FY2025 from {fy24.take_rate} in FY2024 even as GOV grew to {fy25.gov}, commission monetisation is holding up under pressure, though the price war (Industry & News Pack, p.4) remains the dominant near-term swing factor for sector margins. This memo does not assert a specific market-share position for any player absent a disclosed source.

financialAnalysis.unitEconomics: Orders grew from {fy23.orders} (FY2023A) to {fy24.orders} (FY2024A) to {fy25.orders} (FY2025A, +{calc.ordersGrowthFy25} YoY), against average order value of {fy23.aov}, {fy24.aov} and {fy25.aov} respectively, GMV compounded from {fy23.gmv} to {fy24.gmv} to {fy25.gmv}. Take rate (commission revenue / GMV) rose from {fy23.take_rate} to {fy24.take_rate} to {fy25.take_rate}, lifting commission revenue to {fy25.commission_revenue} even as net revenue growth decelerated to +{calc.netRevGrowthFy25}, the gap between a rising take rate and slowing net-revenue growth is the price-war signature: Jahez is monetising a larger share of a GMV base that is itself growing more slowly.
financialAnalysis.operatingLeverage: FY2024A's adjusted EBITDA margin of {fy24.adj_ebitda_margin} on {fy24.net_revenue} of net revenue gave way to {fy25.adj_ebitda_margin} on {fy25.net_revenue} in FY2025A, a ~{calc.ebitdaMarginContractionBps} bps contraction, as depreciation & amortisation grew faster than revenue ({fy24.dna} to {fy25.dna}, reflecting the expanding IFRS-16 right-of-use base) and the {fy25.one_off} of one-offs hit the P&L below the EBITDA line. Segment detail shows the KSA delivery platform still carrying the group: KSA adjusted EBITDA of {fy25.segment_ksa_adj_ebitda} on {fy25.segment_ksa_net_revenue} of net revenue, while Non-KSA, Logistics and Others remain sub-scale or loss-making. The operating-leverage thesis is that fixed platform and logistics costs, once absorbed, let incremental GMV drop through at a materially higher margin than the FY2025A print, which is why the forecast rebuilds margin toward the FY2024A level by the terminal year rather than assuming a permanent step-down.
financialAnalysis.fcfProfile: Free cash flow to the firm is forecast-only in this model, FY2023A-FY2025A capex is not separately disclosed, so no actual-year FCFF feeds the DCF (capex and ΔNWC are documented analyst assumptions; see workbook Assumptions tab). On the explicit forecast, FCFF builds from a depressed FY2026E base as EBITDA margin recovers on the path described above, discounted at a WACC of {calc.wacc} with a {calc.terminalGrowth} terminal growth rate. The DCF sums the explicit-period present value plus a Gordon terminal value into an enterprise value that, net of {calc.netCash} of net cash, implies {calc.targetPrice} per share; full year-by-year FCFF detail is in the workbook's DCF tab.

memo.valuation: The primary methodology is a free-cash-flow-to-firm DCF. The WACC of {calc.wacc} is built bottom-up from a {calc.riskFreeRate} Saudi risk-free rate, a {calc.beta} comparable-set beta, and a {calc.equityRiskPremium} equity risk premium, giving a {calc.costOfEquity} cost of equity; terminal value applies Gordon growth of {calc.terminalGrowth}. The base case yields {calc.targetPrice} per share, {calc.upside} against the {calc.currentPrice} close. Trading-comparables triangulation (EV/Revenue, EV/EBITDA, and P/E across Talabat, DoorDash, and Delivery Hero, per the Market Data & Comparables Snapshot, p.4) spans {calc.compsMin} to {calc.compsMax} of implied value per share (median {calc.compsMedian}); the width of that field reflects the gap between KSA-scale multiples and richly-priced US growth peers, and the DCF result sits toward the lower half of the range, anchored to Jahez's own projected cash flows rather than borrowed multiples. Scenario weighting (25% bull at {calc.perShareBull}, 50% base, 25% bear at {calc.perShareBear}) produces a probability-weighted value of {calc.weightedPerShare} per share and an expected return of {calc.weightedReturn} against the {calc.hurdle} hurdle.
memo.riskIntro: The quantified risk register below scores each identified risk on a 1-5 probability x 1-5 impact basis, rolling up to a composite, peak-weighted score of {calc.riskScore} out of 10. The dominant risk is price-war margin compression from Keeta's well-funded discounting, the same dynamic already visible in the {calc.ebitdaMarginContractionBps} bps FY2025A EBITDA-margin contraction and the {calc.q1LossMagnitude} Q1-2026A net loss, followed by the arrival of further well-capitalised entrants. Both are structural, sector-wide pressures rather than Jahez-specific execution failures, and both are explicitly reflected in the bear-case scenario ({calc.irrBear} IRR, {calc.perShareBear} per share) rather than assumed away.
memo.mandateFit: Against the Lunar Investment Mandate: the scenario-weighted expected return of {calc.weightedReturn} clears the {calc.hurdle} IRR hurdle (Lunar IC Charter, p.3) with the base case alone at {calc.irrBase}; a new Jahez position would be sized within the mandate's 10% single-name concentration cap (Lunar IC Charter, p.4), subject to final ticket sizing at the Investment Committee's discretion; and the position is publicly listed on Tadawul (6017) with active daily trading, satisfying the liquidity requirement for public-equity allocations (Lunar IC Charter, p.4). The bear case ({calc.irrBear} IRR) would breach the {calc.hurdle} hurdle in isolation, the mandate-fit case rests on the {calc.weightedReturn} probability-weighted return, not the base case alone, and the Investment Committee should weigh that sensitivity explicitly.
memo.compliance: Jahez passes Lunar's AAOIFI/Tadawul-methodology compliance screen on both financial-ratio tests: interest-bearing debt, {q1_26.islamic_facilities_loans} of Islamic facilities and loans (Q1 2026 Financial Statements), sits at {calc.debtRatio} of market capitalisation, and cash & interest-bearing securities, {q1_26.cash} of cash (Q1 2026 Financial Statements; interest-bearing securities are not separately disclosed, so the screen is run on cash alone), sits at {calc.cashRatio}, both comfortably inside the 33% AAOIFI leverage/liquidity threshold. Including IFRS-16 lease liabilities ({q1_26.lease_liabilities}) in the stricter memo view still passes at {calc.leaseInclRatio}. Non-eligible income is not separately disclosed in the filings; per AAOIFI practice the position is screened via the business-activity test instead, food delivery and logistics is an eligible business activity, financed via Islamic facilities, rather than a fabricated income percentage. Overall Compliance screen: {calc.complianceStatus}.
memo.catalysts: Near-term catalysts: Snoonu's first full financial year of consolidation (the FY2025 contribution was {fy25.snoonu_gmv_contribution} of Group GMV from an October start; a full FY2026 should show materially more), the path back to positive net income after the {calc.q1LossMagnitude} Q1-2026A loss, and the trajectory of take rate ({fy25.take_rate} in FY2025A) as a read on how much of the Keeta-led price war Jahez can pass through versus absorb. The Investment Committee should treat any further one-off impairment (Q4-2025A already carried {fy25.one_off} across Red Color, Marn goodwill, Others-segment ECL and Snoonu deal costs) as a trigger to re-run the scenario weights, not as one-time noise to dismiss.
monitoringKpis: GMV growth, {fy25.gmv} FY2025A (+{calc.gmvGrowthFy25} YoY); watch for deceleration below the FY2026E-FY2030E forecast path
Take rate, {fy25.take_rate} FY2025A vs {fy24.take_rate} FY2024A; the primary lever on net revenue given a competitive GMV environment
Adjusted EBITDA margin, {fy25.adj_ebitda_margin} FY2025A vs {fy24.adj_ebitda_margin} FY2024A; the recovery path is the single biggest swing factor in the valuation
Net income / net loss, {fy25.net_income} FY2025A, {q1_26.net_loss} Q1-2026A; watch for a return to consistent profitability
Cash & net cash position, {q1_26.cash} cash, {calc.netCash} net cash as of Q1-2026; supports the compliance screen and funds continued logistics build-out
Snoonu contribution, {fy25.snoonu_gmv_contribution} of FY2025A Group GMV from an October consolidation start; the full-year FY2026 read-through is the key diversification proof point
